results.possibleIdentity: Daniel T. Smoley (born c. 1949) was an electronic engineer and technician based in New York City during the late 1960s and early 1970s. He was a professional in the field of digital circuit design and electronic production, employed by Photobell Inc., a company located at 12 East 22nd Street in Manhattan that specialized in photoelectric controls and industrial electronic equipment. Smoley was an active participant in the New York art and activist community of the era; he was a member of the Art Workers' Coalition (AWC) and was among the signatories of a prominent 1970 open letter from the Artists and Writers Protest group to Pablo Picasso, urging the artist to withdraw his painting 'Guernica' from the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA) as a protest against the Vietnam War. His technical expertise in electronic design and testing led to his involvement with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), a non-profit organization that bridged the gap between the technical and artistic communities. He was registered in the E.A.T. Technical Services program, which matched engineers with artists to facilitate the creation of technologically advanced artworks.
